plang.note: Corrected after clean rebuild. Initial run reported 176/181 — that was a stale `PlangConsole/bin/.../plang` binary pre-dating be77dc12. See tester/v1/correction.md. Original (stale) numbers preserved below for traceability.

coverage.note: dotnet test --collect coverage blocked on .NET 10 / Microsoft.Testing.Platform (VSTest path unsupported). Skipped quantitative coverage; analysis below is structural.

findings.issue: Coder v2 summary claims 'PLang tests: 181/181 pass'. Actual: 176/181 — five failures: Audit, CauseLink, CrossFileChain, TagBareLabelWritesTrue, TagWritesPairsOntoCurrentCall. The branch is NOT ready to merge.
findings.impact: Branch close-out is blocked. The coder's claim is contradicted by `cd Tests && plang --test`. Either the coder ran tests from a different directory, didn't run them, or relied on stale output.
findings.suggestion: Re-run the suite the way CLAUDE.md prescribes (`cd Tests && plang --test`) before reporting status. Then fix the failures (see findings 2-4) before claiming the branch is mergeable.

findings.issue: Both tag tests fail with 'Action \'debug.tag\' not found'. The handler exists in `PLang/App/modules/debug/tag.cs` (Action attribute = "tag", namespace = `App.modules.debug`) and the .pr files correctly reference module=debug, action=tag — yet runtime cannot resolve it. The discovery/registration path is broken for the `debug` module.
findings.impact: Every PLang program using `- tag ...` fails at runtime. The architect/test-designer Phase 8 surface (`debug.tag`) is unreleased.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime smoke test that the action manager actually exposes `debug.tag`, then trace why the existing module discovery skips it. Likely candidates: assembly scan filter, module name normalization (case?), or a missing entry in a static module registry. The C# `TagActionTests` may be exercising the handler directly bypassing the dispatch lookup — verify by adding an end-to-end test that goes through the same lookup the runtime uses.

findings.issue: Audit.test.goal: `set %auditCount% = %!callStack.Audit.Count%` produces `(null)` — the Audit collection isn't reachable from PLang via the `%!callStack.Audit` path. CauseLink.test.goal: same null at `%!callStack.Audit[0].Cause.Module%`-style access. CrossFileChain.test.goal: Cyclic %var% reference detected while resolving `%!callStack.Audit%`. Three independent callers, single root cause.
findings.impact: The Audit accumulator and recovery Cause linkage — both architect-Phase deliverables — are invisible to PLang code. Any goal that reads `%!callStack.Audit%` either gets null or a cyclic-reference exception. The C# unit tests pass because they exercise the accumulator directly; the PLang surface that user code actually uses is broken.
findings.suggestion: Add an integration test under `Tests/App/CallStack/` that exercises `%!callStack.Audit.Count%` with a simple known-N error scenario (no foreach, no propagation arithmetic) so the binding is verified independently of the count formula. Then fix the binding. The Cyclic %var% error in CrossFileChain suggests the Audit property may resolve back through a structure that contains itself (each Call carries its own Errors and the chain walk-up re-reads the live tree).

findings.issue: Comment justifies count=7 with `4 inner throws (each handled) + 1 fourth throw + 2 propagation frames for the fourth (goal.call ThrowItem and the foreach goal.call) = 7`. The arithmetic double-counts the fourth throw — the '4 inner throws' already includes the unhandled one, then '+1 fourth throw' adds it again. The expected number reads like it was retrofitted to a hoped-for implementation rather than derived. Even when the binding is fixed, this test will pass for the wrong reason or fail for an arithmetic mismatch.
findings.impact: Future refactors of the Audit semantics can't use this test as a contract — the formula isn't honest.
findings.suggestion: Drop the multi-iteration foreach and the propagation arithmetic. Write three small goals: (a) one handled throw → Audit.Count == 1; (b) one unhandled throw caught at outer → Audit.Count == 2 (inner observation + propagation); (c) chain depth 3 with one throw → Audit.Count == 3. Each test assigns a single, derivable number with a comment explaining the rule, not the arithmetic.

findings.issue: Both 'HandledFlag' tests are named for the Handled flag but never read it. HandledFlagSetWhenRecoverySucceeds asserts `%recovered% is true` and `%!error% is null`. HandledFlagFalseWhenRecoveryFails asserts `%outerCaught% is true`. These prove control flow reached the recovery body; they don't prove the Call.Handled flag was set/unset. A subtle bug that recovers the value but leaves Handled=false (or vice versa) passes both tests.
findings.impact: The Handled flag is the contract observers (debug, audit aggregation, telemetry) read to know whether a frame's error was suppressed. Tests named for it but not exercising it are false greens.
findings.suggestion: Add a step that reads the flag through PLang: e.g. `assert %!callStack.Audit[0].Handled% is true` (success case) / `is false` (recovery-failed case). Once Audit binding is fixed (finding 3) this becomes testable. Until then, capture the flag through whatever path the runtime exposes — even a synthetic `set %h% = %!callStack.Current.Handled%` would tell us more than the current proxy assertions.

findings.issue: Two goals (TestHandledFlagFalseWhenRecoveryFails + InnerWithFailingRecovery) in one .goal file. CLAUDE.md (and tester memory) explicitly says 'One goal per .goal file — multi-goal files get overwritten by the builder.' It happens to build cleanly here because the test goal is the first goal, but it's a latent foot-gun.
findings.impact: A future builder rebuild that picks this file alphabetically with a different ordering rule could overwrite one of the goals.
findings.suggestion: Extract InnerWithFailingRecovery to its own file (e.g. `InnerWithFailingRecovery.goal`) like the other CallStack helpers (CaptureCause.goal, ChainOuter.goal, ThrowItem.goal already follow this pattern).

findings.issue: Cause-boundary annotation tests (Render_CauseBoundary_*, Render_DescendantsInsideRecovery_*) only cover the structural shape with newly-pushed Calls. The risk register in coder v2 summary explicitly flags 'Cause annotation reference equality breaks if Cause becomes a virtual property that reconstructs.' No test pins the reference-equality assumption — a regression that returns a new Cause instance on each access would silently turn off all cause annotations and no test would catch it.
findings.impact: If a future refactor touches Call.Cause's getter, the renderer's IsCauseBoundary becomes a no-op and no annotation regression test fails.
findings.suggestion: Add a single test that calls `recover.Cause` twice and asserts `ReferenceEquals(call1, call2)` — pins the contract the renderer depends on. Or change the renderer to compare by content (Cause.Action.Step == ...) and drop the reference dep.

findings.issue: Test sets DynamicData with `() => "boom"` and asserts the resolved value equals "boom". Strong as far as it goes — but doesn't pin the negative direction. A regression that re-skipped %!*% but happened to return the placeholder evaluated against a different lookup (e.g. variables.Get returning the live value) would still produce "boom" if the test variable name happens to collide. Add an explicit negative: assert the placeholder text `%!error%` is NOT present in the result, mirroring the LlmFixer-shape test in DataAsTResolutionTests.
findings.impact: Low — the test still catches the documented regression class. But the symmetric DataAsTResolutionTests file has positive+negative pairs and this one doesn't.

summary: Corrected after env-divergence root-cause: 181/181 PLang on clean rebuild. Findings 1-3 (the five 'critical' failures) were artefacts of a stale `PlangConsole/bin/.../plang` binary inherited across bot sessions, pre-dating `be77dc12`'s `app.modules.debug.tag` and `%!callStack.Audit` wiring. Coder/v2's claim was honest. C# suite 2623/2623 throughout. Findings 4-8 (test-quality / convention) stand on their own as minor cleanups, not blockers. See tester/v1/correction.md.
